narrative_summary:
The BRCA2 c.1792A>G (p.Thr598Ala, T598A) is a missense change in exon 10 of the breast‑cancer susceptibility gene BRCA2 that has been evaluated in the context of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer risk. Early epidemiological work noted that the variant was not observed in a control cohort and, together with other criteria, was concluded to be non‑deleterious [12215251]. Subsequent clinical re‑classification efforts have repeatedly downgraded the variant from a VUS to likely benign or benign, citing lack of co‑segregation with disease in three unrelated families, occurrence in cis with truncating BRCA2 mutations, and absence of enrichment in cancer cases [PMC6319008; PMC7244490; PMC8160182; PMC4558085; PMC9633799]. However, a few studies have reported the variant as a VUS, noting conflicting interpretations in the literature and limited segregation data, for example in a Bangladeshi cohort and a population‑based young‑onset breast cancer series [PMC6721087; PMC2374975].

Functional assays provide mixed signals. High‑throughput functional evaluation using the MANO‑B assay classified T598A as normal (fClass 1) and therefore benign [PMC7244490], and other in‑silico functional pipelines similarly labeled it benign or neutral [PMC8160182; PMC9988940]. In contrast, a mouse embryonic stem cell‑based functional model reported impaired activity for T598A, supporting a pathogenic interpretation [PMC7722754].

Population data show the variant at low frequency across diverse ancestries (e.g., 0.019 in a Slovenian cohort, 0.0037 in a Polish series, 0.0023 in gnomAD, and similar frequencies in Chinese, Indian and South‑Asian datasets) without a clear excess in affected individuals, consistent with a rare polymorphism rather than a high‑penetrance pathogenic allele [PMC3025939; PMC7443769; PMC3984285; PMC4891063; PMC8753086].

Computational predictions overwhelmingly support a benign effect: PolyPhen‑2, SIFT, REVEL, CADD, iFish, AlphaFold‑based stability scores, and splice‑prediction tools all assign benign or tolerated scores, and the variant is listed as benign/likely benign in ClinVar and other curated databases [PMC8160182; PMC9988940; PMC4985647; PMC9980257].

Overall, the preponderance of clinical re‑classifications, functional assays indicating normal activity, low population frequencies without disease enrichment, and concordant benign computational predictions argue that BRCA2 T598A is most appropriately considered a benign or likely benign polymorphism. The isolated reports of functional impairment and VUS classification represent minority findings that have not been substantiated by larger datasets or replicated functional studies, and thus do not outweigh the weight of evidence supporting a non‑pathogenic status.

mentions.quoted_text: Many missense variants identified in BRCA1 and BRCA2, two genes responsible for the majority of hereditary breast and ovarian cancer, are of unclear clinical significance. Characterizing the significance of such variants is important for medical management of patients in whom they are identified. The aim of this study was to characterize eight of the most common reported missense mutations in BRCA1 and BRCA2 occurring in patients tested for hereditary risk of breast and ovarian cancers. The prevalence of each variant in a control population, co-segregation of the variant with cancer within families, location of the variant within the gene, the nature of the amino acid substitution and conservation of the wild-type amino acid among species were considered. In a control population, the BRCA1 variants M1652I, R1347G, and S1512I, were each observed at a frequency of 4.08%, 2.04%, and 2.04%, respectively, and the BRCA2 variants A2951T, V2728I, and D1420Y, were seen at 1.02%, 0.68%, and 0.34%, respectively. Although the BRCA2 variants **T598A** and R2034C were not seen in this group of controls, other clinical and published observations indicate that these variants are not deleterious. Based on epidemiological and biological criteria, we therefore conclude that the BRCA1 missense mutations R1347G, S1512I and M1652I, and the BRCA2 missense mutations **T598A**, D1420Y, R2034C, V2728I, and A2951T, are not deleterious mutations.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study concludes that the BRCA2 T598A missense variant is not deleterious (benign) based on epidemiological and biological criteria.
article_mentions.title: Characterization of common BRCA1 and BRCA2 variants.

mentions.quoted_text: Forty-five variants classified as P, LP or VUS were identified among the 158 tested patients according to commercial laboratories reports. After reviewing and reclassifying all P, LP variants and VUS according to ACMG Guidelines we found conflicting interpretation in 8 variants. In BRCA1, the variant c.67_75delGAGTGTCCC (p.Glu23_Pro25del) first classified as VUS was reclassified as LP, based on experimental analysis of three tumors from this patient that showed loss-of-heterozygosity for the normal BRCA1 allele (data not shown). The variant c.4964C > T (p.Ser1655Phe) first classified as P was reclassified as LP, the variant c.2368A > G (p.Thr790Ala) and c.1067A > G (p.Gln356Arg) both first classified as VUS were reclassified as LB and B respectively. In BRCA2 the variants c.794-22C > T, c.7601A > G (p.Ala2534Val), c.**1792A > G** (**p.Thr598Ala**), and c.4928 T > C (p.Val1643Ala), first classified as VUS were all reclassified as LB.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study evaluates breast cancer risk of rare missense variants in several susceptibility genes and finds that only a very small proportion of BRCA2 missense variants may confer risk comparable to truncating variants, while most show no significant association, as exemplified by the non‑significant OR for BRCA2 p.Thr598Ala.
article_mentions.title: Breast cancer risks associated with missense variants in breast cancer susceptibility genes
